pertinent_positives.addl-hx: pill-rolling 4-6 Hz rest tremor worse at rest and improved with movement, micrographia, shuffling gait, reduced sense of smell, failure to respond to sertraline
pertinent_positives.pe: masked facies, reduced blink rate, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing, positive pull test
pertinent_positives.labs: normal CBC, CMP, TSH ruling out metabolic and thyroid causes
pertinent_positives.imaging: MRI brain without acute findings, no midbrain atrophy
pertinent_positives.special: DaTscan showing reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamen consistent with nigrostriatal degeneration

report.plan: initiate levodopa/carbidopa 25/100mg TID, taper sertraline, refer to movement disorder specialist, occupational therapy referral, outpatient follow-up in 4-6 weeks

explanation.who_gets_it: Adults over 60, slightly more common in men. Can occur younger with genetic mutations (LRRK2, PINK1, Parkin). Drug-induced parkinsonism mimics it — caused by dopamine blockers like haloperidol and metoclopramide.
explanation.risk_factors: Age, male sex, pesticide/herbicide exposure, rural living, well water. Family history (LRRK2 most common genetic cause). Protective: smoking, caffeine (epidemiologically).
explanation.typical_presentation: Rest tremor (pill-rolling, 4-6 Hz, disappears with movement), bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, postural instability. Non-motor: hyposmia, constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, depression, micrographia, hypophonic voice, masked facies.
explanation.moa: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta → reduced dopamine in the striatum → unopposed cholinergic activity → movement disorder. Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein aggregates) are the pathologic hallmark found on autopsy.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Rest tremor, pill-rolling, cogwheel rigidity, masked facies, shuffling gait, micrographia, substantia nigra, Lewy bodies, dopamine deficiency, DaTscan comma-to-period sign.
explanation.lab_buzzwords: DaTscan: reduced dopamine transporter uptake in putamen — asymmetric, comma becomes period. MRI brain normal on standard sequences. Clinical diagnosis — DaTscan confirms when uncertain.
explanation.treatment: Levodopa/Carbidopa (gold standard). MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline) for mild/early disease. Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole). NEVER give dopamine blockers (haloperidol, metoclopramide, promethazine). DBS for refractory cases.

report.pmh: hypertension, GERD, and 45 pack-year smoking history
report.presenting: a 3-month progressive cough with hemoptysis and 12 lb unintentional weight loss

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: progressive dry cough, two episodes of hemoptysis, 12 lb weight loss despite normal appetite, morning facial swelling worse with bending forward, collar too tight, morning headaches, proximal muscle weakness, diplopia
pertinent_positives.pe: plethoric cyanotic facies, bilateral upper extremity and facial edema, dilated non-pulsatile neck veins, chest wall venous collaterals flowing downward, decreased right upper lobe breath sounds, proximal muscle weakness with diminished reflexes
pertinent_positives.labs: hyponatremia Na 128 with inappropriately elevated urine Na consistent with SIADH, normocytic anemia Hgb 10.4, mildly elevated LFTs
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR and CT chest showing 6.2cm right hilar mass with 80% SVC compression and extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy
pertinent_positives.special: bronchoscopy biopsy confirming small cell carcinoma, Ki-67 >80%, synaptophysin and chromogranin A positive, extensive stage

report.assessment: Small Cell Lung Cancer, Extensive Stage — complicated by SVC syndrome, SIADH, and Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome
report.plan: admit for stabilization, head of bed elevation, IV dexamethasone, fluid restriction 800-1000 mL/day for SIADH, urgent oncology consult, expedite tissue diagnosis confirmation, initiate carboplatin/etoposide/atezolizumab upon histologic confirmation

explanation.who_gets_it: Heavy smokers (>30 pack-years), typically men over 60. SCLC ~15% of all lung cancers, most aggressive subtype.
explanation.risk_factors: Smoking (>95% of cases). Radon, asbestos, arsenic, chromium. Prior thoracic radiation.
explanation.typical_presentation: Central hilar mass, rapid doubling time, early mediastinal spread. Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (ectopic ADH), Lambert-Eaton (proximal weakness with facilitation — VGCC antibodies), Cushing (ectopic ACTH). SVC syndrome: facial plethora, arm edema, non-pulsatile neck veins.
explanation.moa: Neuroendocrine tumor from Kulchitsky cells. Expresses synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56, TTF-1. Ki-67 >50%. Ectopic hormone production drives paraneoplastic syndromes.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Small blue cells, salt-and-pepper chromatin, nuclear molding, neuroendocrine markers, SIADH, Lambert-Eaton (facilitation on EMG — opposite of MG), SVC syndrome, central hilar mass, never surgical in extensive stage.
explanation.lab_buzzwords: Hyponatremia + inappropriately high urine Na = SIADH. Lambert-Eaton: facilitation on high-frequency repetitive stimulation. Low voltage EKG with large mediastinal mass.
explanation.treatment: Extensive stage: Carboplatin + Etoposide + Atezolizumab (IMpower133). Limited stage: concurrent chemoradiation + prophylactic cranial irradiation. No surgery in extensive stage. Highly chemo-sensitive initially — relapses almost universally within 1-2 years.

report.pmh: bicuspid aortic valve diagnosed at age 22, no prior interventions
report.presenting: 3 weeks of daily fevers, night sweats, and 9 lb weight loss following a dental cleaning without antibiotic prophylaxis

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: dental procedure 5 weeks ago without prophylaxis, known bicuspid aortic valve, pleuritic chest pain, painful erythematous nodules on fingertip pads, dark lines under fingernails, yellow eyes noted by wife
pertinent_positives.pe: new harsh diastolic murmur grade III/VI, wide pulse pressure 122/48, Osler nodes on right index and middle fingertips, splinter hemorrhages under nails, Janeway lesions on bilateral thenar eminences, Roth spots on fundoscopy, splenomegaly 2cm below costal margin
pertinent_positives.labs: leukocytosis WBC 16,800 with left shift, normocytic anemia Hgb 9.8, elevated creatinine 1.6, microscopic hematuria with RBC casts on UA, new PR prolongation to 220ms on EKG
pertinent_positives.imaging: TTE showing 1.4cm vegetation on bicuspid aortic valve with severe aortic regurgitation, bilateral peripheral nodular opacities on CXR consistent with septic emboli
pertinent_positives.special: all 3 blood culture sets positive for Streptococcus viridans, fully penicillin susceptible MIC 0.06

report.assessment: Infective Endocarditis — native bicuspid aortic valve, Streptococcus viridans, complicated by severe aortic regurgitation, immune complex glomerulonephritis, septic pulmonary emboli, and new PR prolongation concerning for perivalvular abscess
report.plan: blood cultures x3 before antibiotics, IV ceftriaxone 2g daily x4 weeks, urgent TEE to evaluate for perivalvular abscess given PR prolongation, cardiac surgery consult, admit to cardiology with surgery on standby, serial EKGs and echo

explanation.who_gets_it: Structural valve disease (bicuspid AV, MVP, rheumatic), IV drug users (tricuspid — S. aureus), prosthetic valves, recent dental/invasive procedures without prophylaxis.
explanation.risk_factors: Structural heart disease, IV drug use, poor dentition, central venous catheters, immunosuppression, hemodialysis, prior endocarditis.
explanation.typical_presentation: Fever + new murmur = endocarditis until proven otherwise. Osler nodes (tender, fingertips — immune complex), Janeway lesions (painless, palms/soles — septic emboli), splinter hemorrhages, Roth spots. Embolic complications: stroke, renal infarct, septic pulmonary emboli. Immune complex GN: RBC casts on UA.
explanation.moa: Bacteremia seeds damaged endothelium → vegetation (platelets + fibrin + bacteria) → valve destruction + systemic emboli + immune complex deposition. Strep viridans post-dental. S. aureus most aggressive. Enterococcus from GI/GU.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Duke criteria (2 major OR 1 major + 3 minor OR 5 minor), Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, Roth spots, splinter hemorrhages, new murmur, positive blood cultures, vegetation on echo. PR prolongation → perivalvular abscess.
explanation.lab_buzzwords: Blood cultures x3 before antibiotics. UA: RBC casts = immune complex GN. New PR prolongation = perivalvular abscess → urgent TEE. Echo vegetation = major Duke criterion.
explanation.treatment: Native Strep viridans (susceptible): IV Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone x4 weeks. S. aureus MSSA: Nafcillin x6 weeks. MRSA: Vancomycin. Surgery: heart failure from valve destruction, perivalvular abscess, uncontrolled infection, recurrent emboli. Prophylaxis: amoxicillin before dental procedures in high-risk valve patients.

report.presenting: episodic attacks of severe headache, diaphoresis, and palpitations over 8 months, with a collapse this morning

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: paroxysmal episodes triggered by exertion and bending lasting 20-40 minutes with complete resolution between attacks, 8 lb weight loss, family history of adrenal tumor in mother
pertinent_positives.pe: BP 188/112, HR 108, flushed and diaphoretic, right upper quadrant abdominal tenderness, hypertensive retinopathy
pertinent_positives.labs: hyperglycemia glucose 148 without prior diabetes, mildly elevated troponin from catecholamine-induced myocardial injury, frequent PVCs on EKG
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT abdomen showing 4.8cm heterogeneous right adrenal mass with central necrosis
pertinent_positives.special: plasma free metanephrines 2.8 nmol/L and 24-hr urine metanephrines markedly elevated, confirming biochemical diagnosis

report.assessment: Pheochromocytoma — right adrenal gland, biochemically confirmed
report.plan: admit for alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine, endocrinology and surgical oncology consult, 10-14 day preoperative preparation with high-salt diet and volume expansion, genetic testing for RET/VHL/SDHB, laparoscopic adrenalectomy after adequate preparation

explanation.who_gets_it: Any age, peak 40-60. ~40% associated with genetic syndromes: MEN2A/2B (RET), VHL, NF1, SDH mutations. Rule of 10s: 10% malignant, 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% in children.
explanation.risk_factors: MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDH mutations. Family history of adrenal tumors.
explanation.typical_presentation: Classic triad: episodic headache, diaphoresis, palpitations. Paroxysmal hypertension triggered by exertion, bending, stress. Episodes 20-40 minutes with complete resolution between. Weight loss, hyperglycemia, pallor during attacks.
explanation.moa: Chromaffin cell tumor secretes catecholamines → alpha-1 (vasoconstriction, HTN) + beta-1 (tachycardia) + beta-2 (hyperglycemia). Episodes triggered by tumor manipulation or sympathetic activation.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Rule of 10s, episodic HTN, headache/sweating/palpitations, alpha-block before beta-block, never biopsy, plasma free metanephrines (most sensitive), adrenal mass on CT.
explanation.lab_buzzwords: Plasma free metanephrines >0.5 nmol/L. 24-hr urine metanephrines >900 mcg. Hyperglycemia without diabetes. CT: heterogeneous adrenal mass with central necrosis.
explanation.treatment: 1. Alpha-blockade first: Phenoxybenzamine x10-14 days. 2. Beta-blocker only after alpha established. 3. High-salt diet + IV fluids. 4. Laparoscopic adrenalectomy. NEVER: biopsy, beta-block first, operate unprepared. Genetic testing post-op for all.

report.presenting: three weeks of progressive headache, nausea, and cognitive slowing — worse at work, better on days off — with a near-syncopal episode yesterday

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: symptoms strictly worse at work and resolve on days off, two co-workers with identical symptoms, ventilation sealed for winter, near-syncope yesterday
pertinent_positives.pe: encephalopathic, cherry-red skin tone on lips and nail beds, ataxia on tandem gait, tachycardic
pertinent_positives.labs: elevated lactate 4.2, elevated troponin from CO myocardial injury, metabolic acidosis, elevated hematocrit from compensatory polycythemia, normal SpO2 falsely reassuring
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head without acute intracranial process, subtle cerebral edema
pertinent_positives.special: COHb 28% confirming carbon monoxide poisoning — normal SpO2 was misleading

report.assessment: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning — occupational, chronic subacute exposure with acute-on-chronic episode, complicated by myocardial injury and encephalopathy
report.plan: 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask, admit for cardiac monitoring, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, serial troponins, neurology follow-up for delayed sequelae, mandatory reporting and evacuation of furnace hall

explanation.who_gets_it: Anyone in poorly ventilated space with combustion. Industrial workers, faulty heaters, house fires. CO is odorless and colorless. Winter risk highest when ventilation is sealed.
explanation.risk_factors: Faulty heating systems, industrial furnaces with sealed ventilation, enclosed combustion, house fires, charcoal grills indoors.
explanation.typical_presentation: Headache, nausea, confusion — improve away from source and worsen on return. Multiple people affected simultaneously. Cherry-red skin (late/severe). SpO2 falsely normal — dangerous trap. Near-syncope or LOC in severe cases.
explanation.moa: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → COHb → impaired O2 delivery. Also binds myoglobin and cytochrome c oxidase → direct cellular toxicity. SpO2 cannot distinguish COHb from OHb.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Cherry-red skin, normal SpO2 (false), elevated COHb, symptoms improve away from source, multiple people affected, elevated lactate despite normal pO2, bilateral globus pallidus on MRI (delayed sequelae).
explanation.lab_buzzwords: COHb on co-oximetry (ABG) — NOT pulse oximetry. Elevated lactate. Normal pO2 on ABG. Elevated troponin if cardiac involvement. Polycythemia in chronic exposure.
explanation.treatment: 100% O2 via NRB (COHb half-life 5hr → 60-90min). HBO if: COHb >25%, LOC, neurological symptoms, cardiac involvement, pregnancy (half-life ~20min, prevents delayed sequelae). Remove from source. Evacuate all others.

report.pmh: prior spontaneous left pneumothorax 18 months ago, not formally treated
report.presenting: sudden left-sided chest pain and dyspnea at rest — second episode of spontaneous pneumothorax

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: second ipsilateral episode, first ignored 18 months ago, tall lean habitus BMI 19, no trauma or exertion precipitant
pertinent_positives.pe: decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance left hemithorax, trachea midline, O2 95%
pertinent_positives.labs: mild hypoxemia pO2 78 on ABG, low voltage on EKG left leads consistent with PTX
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR showing 30% left pneumothorax with visible pleural line
pertinent_positives.special: CT chest confirming multiple bilateral subpleural blebs, largest 1.4cm left upper lobe

report.assessment: Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax — second episode, bilateral bleb disease on CT, surgical intervention indicated
report.plan: needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube, supplemental O2, admit for observation and repeat CXR, thoracic surgery consult for VATS pleurodesis, Marfan syndrome assessment

explanation.who_gets_it: Primary: tall thin young men (18-40) without lung disease. Secondary: underlying lung disease (COPD, CF, Marfan, TB). Recurrence ~30% after first, >80% after second ipsilateral episode.
explanation.risk_factors: Tall lean habitus, male sex, smoking (secondary). Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, CF, COPD, TB. Primary PSP: often no identifiable risk factor.
explanation.typical_presentation: Sudden sharp pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea at rest — 'pop' sensation. Decreased breath sounds and hyperresonance. Trachea midline unless tension. Tension PTX: tracheal deviation away from affected side + hypotension — immediate needle decompression.
explanation.moa: Rupture of subpleural bleb → air enters pleural space → lung collapses → V/Q mismatch. Tension: one-way valve effect → progressive air accumulation → mediastinal shift → obstructive shock.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Tall thin young man, sudden pleuritic chest pain, decreased breath sounds + hyperresonance, pleural line on CXR, tension = tracheal deviation away + hypotension (immediate 2nd ICS MCL needle).
explanation.lab_buzzwords: CXR: visible pleural line, absent lung markings peripherally. Low voltage EKG ipsilateral leads. ABG: mild hypoxemia. CT: blebs, quantifies collapse.
explanation.treatment: Small (<20%) asymptomatic: observation + O2. Symptomatic or >20%: needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube. Second ipsilateral: VATS bullectomy + pleurodesis. Tension: immediate needle decompression → chest tube. Rule out Marfan in young recurrent PSP.

report.presenting: acute anticholinergic toxidrome following two hours of unprotected dermal exposure to Atropa belladonna during herbalist work

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: two hours bare-handed grinding of belladonna berries and leaves, facial contact, blurred vision then dry mouth then palpitations then urinary retention
pertinent_positives.pe: fixed maximally dilated pupils 8mm bilaterally, flushed hot dry skin with complete anhidrosis, HR 128, temp 38.6, distended bladder 680mL, absent bowel sounds
pertinent_positives.labs: mildly elevated glucose, QT prolongation to 480ms on EKG
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head without acute intracranial process, bladder scan confirming urinary retention
pertinent_positives.special: serum and urine toxicology positive for atropine and tropane alkaloids confirming belladonna anticholinergic toxidrome

report.assessment: Anticholinergic Toxidrome — Atropa belladonna dermal and mucosal exposure, complicated by urinary retention, ileus, hyperthermia, and QT prolongation
report.plan: IV benzodiazepines, urinary catheterization, active cooling, continuous telemetry, IV magnesium on standby, physostigmine if refractory, toxicology consult, admit for 24-48hr monitoring

explanation.who_gets_it: Anyone exposed to muscarinic blockers: belladonna/jimsonweed, atropine, scopolamine, diphenhydramine, TCAs, antipsychotics. Herbalists, children eating berries, medication overdose.
explanation.risk_factors: Occupational exposure to belladonna plants, jimsonweed ingestion, TCA or antihistamine overdose, elderly on multiple anticholinergic medications.
explanation.typical_presentation: Dry as a bone (anhidrosis), blind as a bat (mydriasis), red as a beet (flushing), hot as a hare (hyperthermia), mad as a hatter (delirium). Plus: tachycardia, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds, QT prolongation.
explanation.moa: Competitive antagonism of muscarinic ACh receptors → blocked parasympathetic → tachycardia (vagal block), mydriasis, dry secretions, urinary retention, ileus, hyperthermia (anhidrosis), delirium.
explanation.step1_buzzwords: Dry hot flushed skin (vs sympathomimetic = wet), fixed dilated pupils, tachycardia, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds, delirium, hyperthermia. KEY DISTINCTION: DRY vs sympathomimetic (wet).
explanation.lab_buzzwords: QT prolongation — risk of Torsades. Mildly elevated glucose. Urine/serum toxicology for tropane alkaloids. Bladder scan confirming retention.
explanation.treatment: Supportive: benzodiazepines (NOT haloperidol), urinary catheter, cooling, IV fluids. Antidote: Physostigmine IV (reversible AChE inhibitor, crosses BBB) for severe cases. Give slowly — bradycardia/seizures risk. NEVER atropine. NEVER haloperidol. Monitor QT continuously.
